purpose: Cross-repo README staleness contract. Surveyed weekly by .github/workflows/readme-drift-sensor.yml; each snapshot is consumed by ix-quality-trend as a quality signal and by an issue-bot that opens/updates a single tracking issue in ga when any repo crosses the 'stale' threshold. Mechanism documented in docs/methodology/readme-drift.md.
established_by: feat/readme-drift-sensor (response to user observation 2026-05-17: 'README.md on all repos don't look up to date')
metric_description: Number of tracked sibling repos whose README hasn't been touched within `thresholds_days.stale` of the HEAD commit. LOWER is better — 0 means every sibling's README is within the freshness window.
primary_baseline_source: Hand-survey 2026-05-17 — ga (52d), Demerzel (42d), tars (42d), guitaralchemist.github.io (40d) all >30d stale. Setting baseline=3 because PR #254 refreshed ga before this snapshot lands; the remaining 3 are the in-flight refresh queue.

rollback_metadata.note: README drift is a slow-moving signal. The sensor surfaces it via issue + state file; remediation is operator-driven (refresh the README, merge PR, signal naturally improves). No auto-loop yet — that would require a /readme-sync skill that can author content, which is out of scope for v1.

scope_boundary.note: README content is what should drift to match HEAD. The sensor itself (script, workflow, baseline, methodology doc) is protected because changing it without thought breaks the cross-repo signal.

_open_gaps.per_repo_propagation: v1 surveys cross-repo from ga. v2 could propagate the sensor workflow into each sibling so each repo emits its own drift check on its own PRs. Tracked under task-TBD.
_open_gaps.semantic_drift: v1 measures filename-mtime drift only. v2 could detect content drift — e.g., 'README mentions .NET 9 but global.json says .NET 10' — via an LLM-comparator skill.
_open_gaps.auto_remediation: v1 surfaces a tracking issue. v2 could draft auto-PRs (like what we did manually for PRs #254 + #9 + the two pending) via a /readme-sync skill.
